title: NA-Non ULIP
word_text:
NPS for NRI: Complete Guide to National Pension Scheme for NRIs
As a Non-Resident India, the Indian market appears as a promising space to build a sizeable corpus. Amongst others, the National Pension Scheme, also known as the NPS, is an option worth exploring. It is a secure and regulated way to build a retirement corpus in India. Additionally, NPS is worth exploring as an NRI as it comes with underlying investment benefits and tax efficiencies. Keep reading to learn all about NPS for NRI that will help you make an informed decision.
What is NPS for NRI?
The National Pension Scheme for NRI is a government-sponsored pension/retirement scheme that offers an opportunity to build a sizeable corpus in the long run. The scheme is regulated by the Pension Fund Regulatory and Development Authority (PFRDA), as well as the central government.
NPS was introduced to promote savings and investments to help in wealth accumulation for senior citizens planning their retirement. As an NRI, the scheme can be a promising opportunity to start planning a retirement back in the home country. It comes with multiple investment options where you can actively or passively manage your funds. Additionally, under the Income Tax Act 1961, there are impressive tax benefits for individuals contributing to the National Pension Scheme.
Unlike several other retirement schemes, NPS allows you to start planning your retirement at an early age, even with an amount as low as ₹500.
Eligibility Criteria for NRIs to Invest in NPS
The National Pension Scheme is available for both resident and non-resident Indians. While the process of investing in NPS is simple, it is important to first understand the eligibility criteria. Here’s the eligibility criteria for the NPS account for NRIs:
The individual must be an Indian citizen; both resident and Non-Resident Indians (NRIs) are allowed.
The scheme is available for employees in either the public sector, private sector or the organised sector.
The individual must be aged between 18 and 60 years.
The individual must comply with the know your customer (KYC) norms as per the application.
Note: Remember that the National Pension Scheme is not available for Overseas Citizens of India or Persons of Indian Origin. Any individual holding these cards cannot invest in NPS.
Types of NPS Accounts Available for NRIs
The National Pension Scheme is a long-term retirement savings scheme that is available for individuals of different age groups and with different investment capital. This is simply because under NPS, there are two different types of accounts with varying features, benefits and minimum investment requirements. To help you understand the types of NPS accounts available for NRIs better, here’s a detailed guide:
Tier I Account Features and Benefits
The Tier I account under the National Pension Scheme is available for all Indian citizens who invest in the scheme. This account is primarily opened as a pension account or retirement-focused account. Let’s take a look at the key features and benefits of the Tier I account:
Every subscriber of NPS is eligible to open this account.
The minimum amount required to open this type of account is only ₹500, and an annual contribution of only ₹1,000 is required.
Typically, withdrawals under this account are not allowed till the account holder attains the age of 60 years. A partial withdrawal may be allowed in certain cases.
This type of account is mandatory under the National Pension Scheme.
Tier II Account Features and Benefits
The tier II account under the National Pension Scheme is available for individuals who have an active tier I account under the scheme. This is a voluntary savings account. Let’s take a look at the key features and benefits of a tier II account for NRIs:
Any individual with an active tier I account under the NPS scheme can open a tier II account.
The minimum contribution required to open a tier II account is ₹250.
Unlike a Tier I account, this type of account does not have any minimal annual investment requirement.
This type of account is truly flexible; withdrawals can be made at any time.
This type of account is an optional account under the National Pension Scheme.
Take a look at this table to understand the key differences between the two types of accounts:
How Can NRIs Open an NPS Account? Step-by-Step Process
As an NRI, opening an NPS account is a simple process that can be done online. Here’s how NRIs can open an NPS account:
Step 1: Visit the official website of the Pension Fund Regulatory and Development Authority (PFRDA).
Step 2: From the website home page, select the eNPS menu to initiate the registration process.
Step 3: On the new page, select the “Non Resident Indian” option and choose between Non Resident External Account (NRE) or Non Resident Ordinary Account (NRO), along with certain other details like country of residence, bank account number, etc. Submit these details.
Step 4: Upon submission, an NPS registration form appears on the screen. Fill in the form with personal details like name, age, etc.
Step 5: Submit the form along with all the required documents, including a passport-size photograph and signature.
Step 6: Complete the process by making the initial payment of ₹500. Once the payment is complete, a unique Permanent Retirement Account Number (PRAN) is generated.
Step 7: The registration process is complete. Make sure to print out the system-generated form and send it to the Central Record Keeping Agency within 90 days to avoid freezing of the NPS account for NRI.
Note: Remember that an NRI can invest in NPS only through an NRE or NRO account.
Investment Options under NPS for NRIs
The National Pension Scheme is known for its investment flexibility as it allows investors to invest in various assets and securities in the market. Some common examples of investment options under NPS for NRIs include equities, corporate bonds, government securities, etc. NRIs have the option of investing in a mix of securities as well, depending on their risk appetite.
Under NPS, there are two types of fund management schemes available for NRI NPS accounts:
Active Choice: As the name suggests, under this type of fund management scheme, NRIs can actively decide the type of asset class they wish to invest in and also manage their funds.
Auto Choice: Under the Auto Choice fund management scheme, the asset investment is automatically done based on factors like the subscriber's age and investment amount made by the subscriber. This type of fund management is an ideal choice for NRIs who do not want to actively engage in investments.
Tax Benefits for NPS for NRIs
The National Pension Scheme comes with several tax benefits for NRIs under the Income Tax Act, 1961. The easiest way to understand the tax benefits under NPS is to evaluate these benefits under different types of NPS accounts:
Tier I Account Tax Benefits Under Section 80CCD
Tax deduction up to 10% of the total salary can be claimed under section 80CCD(1). However, the maximum limit under this section is ₹1.5 lakhs as per section 80CCE.
An Additional tax deduction up to ₹50,000 can be claimed under section 80CCD(1B).
Note: Remember that these elections are not allowed under the new tax regime.
Tier II Account Tax Benefits Under Section 80C
Contributions made towards this type of account are eligible for tax deductions under Section 80C.
The deduction limit of section 80C is ₹1.5 lakh.
Tax deduction under this type of account can be claimed only when there is a minimum lock-in period of three years.
Note: Kindly remember that NRIs who are filing their taxes in India can get tax benefits, as per the tax regime they choose and the tax slab they fall into.
Withdrawal Rules and Exit Options for NPS for NRIs
The primary purpose of investing in the National Pension Scheme as an NRI is to build a sizeable corpus to live a peaceful life after retirement. Therefore, it is important that you first carefully understand the NPS withdrawal rules for NRI and exit options under the scheme.
Withdrawal at the Age of 60 Years
The subscriber can draw up to 60% as a lump sum amount.
The remaining 40% has to be mandatorily invested in buying an annuity.
In case the total savings amount in the NPS account is less than ₹2,00,000, then the entire amount can be withdrawn at once.
Note: The subscriber can stay invested in the account till the age of 70 years.
Withdrawal Before the Age of 60 Years
A partial withdrawal of only 20% of the total amount is allowed.
The remaining has to be mandatorily invested in buying an annuity.
In case the total savings in the NPS account are less than ₹1,00,00, then the entire amount can be withdrawn at once.
Death of the Subscriber
In case of the death or the subscriber before the maturity period, the appointed nominee can withdraw the entire fund in the NPS account.
Key Takeaways
The National Pension Scheme is a government-backed retirement plan that is regulated by the Pension Fund Regulatory and Development Authority.
The scheme is available for both resident and non-resident Indians between the ages of 18 and 70 years.
Under NPS, there are two types of accounts: Tier I and Tier II.
There are different investment options available for the NRIs under NPS, like equities, corporate bonds, government securities, etc.
Typically, a partial withdrawal before the age of 60 years is not allowed under an NPS account.
Conclusion
The National Pension Scheme is a reliable option for NRIs as it comes with long-term investment options, flexibility and a low minimum investment requirement. As an NRI, several options in India can help juggle financial responsibilities across borders, like term insurance for NRIs or other life insurance plans.
FAQs
Can NRIs open an NPS account without an NRE or NRO account?
No, NRIs cannot open an NPS account without an NRE or NRO account.
Is a tier II NPS account available for NRIs?
Yes, the tier II NPS account is available for NRIs who already have an active tier I account under the National Pension Scheme.
What happens to my NPS account if I become an OCI?
The National Pension Scheme is available only for Indian citizens, and Overseas Citizens of India (OCI) cannot hold an account under the NPS.
Is there any penalty for early withdrawal from NPS for NRIs?
Only 20% of the total fund amount is allowed as an early withdrawal, i.e. before the age of 60 years. However, in case the total fund value is less than ₹1 lakh, then a complete withdrawal is allowed.
How can NRIs contribute to NPS from abroad?
NRIs can contribute to NPS from abroad simply by using either their Non-Resident External (NRE) or Non-Resident Ordinary (NRO) account.
What are the tax implications of NPS corpus withdrawal for NRIs?
NPS corpus withdrawals are tax-free if withdrawn upon superannuation. However, the annuity purchased either before or after superannuation is liable for tax liabilities.
Disclaimer:
The views stated in this article are not to be construed as investment advice and readers are suggested to seek independent financial advice before making any investment decisions. For more details on risk factors, terms and conditions, please read the sales brochure & policy document (available on www.bajajallianzlife.com) carefully before concluding a sale. full_pdf_text:
NPS for NRI: Complete Guide to National Pension Scheme for NRIs
As a Non-Resident India, the Indian market appears as a promising space to build a sizeable corpus.
Amongst others, the National Pension Scheme, also known as the NPS, is an option worth exploring. It is
a secure and regulated way to build a retirement corpus in India. Additionally, NPS is worth exploring as
an NRI as it comes with underlying investment benefits and tax efficiencies. Keep reading to learn all
about NPS for NRI that will help you make an informed decision.
What is NPS for NRI?
The National Pension Scheme for NRI is a market linked retirement scheme that offers an opportunity
to build a corpus in the long run. The scheme is regulated by the Pension Fund Regulatory and
Development Authority (PFRDA).
NPS was introduced to promote savings and market linked investments to help in wealth accumulation
for senior citizens planning their retirement. As an NRI, the scheme can be a promising opportunity to
start planning a retirement back in the home country. It comes with multiple investment options where
you can actively or passively manage your funds. Additionally, under the Income Tax Act 1961, there are
tax benefits for individuals contributing to the National Pension Scheme.
Unlike several other retirement schemes, NPS allows you to start planning your retirement at an early
age, even with an amount as low as ₹500 (for Tier 1 accounts) and ₹250 (for tier 2 accounts)1.
Eligibility Criteria for NRIs to Invest in NPS2
The National Pension Scheme is available for both resident and non-resident Indians. While the process
of investing in NPS is simple, it is important to first understand the eligibility criteria. Here’s the eligibility
criteria for the NPS account for NRIs:
● The individual must be an Indian citizen; both resident and Non-Resident Indians (NRIs) are
allowed.
● The scheme is available for employees in either the public sector, private sector or the organised
sector.
● The individual must be aged between 18 and 70 years3.
● The individual must comply with the know your customer (KYC) norms as per the application.
Note: Remember that the National Pension Scheme is not available for HUFs and Persons of Indian
Origin (PIO3.
Types of NPS Accounts Available for NRIs
The National Pension Scheme is a market linked retirement scheme that is available for individuals of
different age groups and with different investment capital. This is simply because under NPS, there are
two different types of accounts with varying features, benefits and minimum investment requirements.
To help you understand the types of NPS accounts available for NRIs better, here’s a detailed guide:
Tier I Account Features and Benefits
The Tier I account under the National Pension Scheme is available for all Indian citizens who invest in the
scheme. This account is primarily opened as a pension account or retirement-focused account. Let’s take
a look at the key features and benefits of the Tier I account:
● Every subscriber of NPS is eligible to open this account.
● The minimum amount required to open this type of account is only ₹500, and a minimum
annual contribution of only ₹1,000 is required2.
● Typically, withdrawals under this account are allowed only in certain cases.
● This type of account is mandatory under the National Pension Scheme.
Tier II Account Features and Benefits
The tier II account under the National Pension Scheme is available for individuals who have an active tier
I account under the scheme. This is a voluntary savings account. Let’s take a look at the key features and
benefits of a tier II account for NRIs:
● Any individual with an active tier I account under the NPS scheme can open a tier II account.
● The minimum contribution required to open a tier II account is ₹2502.
● Unlike a Tier I account, this type of account does not have any minimal annual investment
requirement.
● This type of account is flexible; withdrawals can be made at any time1.
● This type of account is an optional account under the National Pension Scheme.
Take a look at this table to understand the key differences between the two types of accounts:
Features Tier I Account Tier II Account

in the NPS.

time.
How Can NRIs Open an NPS Account? Step-by-Step Process
As an NRI, opening an NPS account is a simple process that can be done through eNPS using their
Aadhaar Card/ PAN and bank account verification. An Aadhaar Card holder aged 18 to 60, with their
mobile number registered with Aadhaar can open an NPS online.
Here is the step-by-step process.
● Step 1: Getting Started
Visit the official website of the Pension Fund Regulatory and Development Authority (PFRDA).
Choose eNPS”. Click on the “Registration and the “New Registration”. Enter passport and
Aadhaar details, verify with OTP, and provide NRE/NRO bank details (via empanelled bank for
repatriable accounts or self-declaration for non-repatriable). Aadhaar will auto-fill your photo
and personal information, and you just need to complete the remaining fields, as explained
below.
● Step 2: Personal Details
Enter your name, parents’ name, mobile number, and email ID. Your Aadhaar will serve as ID
proof; you just need to add your date of birth and select proof. Click ‘Generate
Acknowledgement No’ to proceed immediately or use it later to continue.
● Step 3: Contact Details
Your address will be fetched from the Aadhaar details. Click on save and proceed.
● Step 4: Bank and Other Details
You now need to choose your occupation and fill in the required details, then enter your bank
account information, ensuring the bank name matches the one selected earlier. For repatriable
accounts, pick from the empanelled banks and provide NRE/NRO details, while for non-
repatriable accounts, you can give any NRE/NRO details on a self-declaration basis. Finally, click
“Save & Proceed” to continue.
● Step 5: Scheme Nomination & Details
Now you need to make your investment choice: Active or Auto. Carefully specify the allocation
percentage. For nominee details, you can add names of up to 3 nominees. Save and Proceed.
● Step 6: Photograph & Signatures
You have the option of choosing a photograph other than the Aadhaar database. You would
have to upload the signature and photograph in .jpg format (between 4kb and 12 kb). Click on
save.
Step 7: Payment
Now you need an initial contribution that can be a minimum of ₹500. Once the payment is made, you
will receive the Permanent Retirement Account Number (PRAN).
Note: For a repatriable eNPS NRI account, all future contributions must come from the bank account
given at registration. For a non-repatriable account, contributions can be made through any mode, net
banking, credit card, or debit card.
Note: Remember that an NRI can invest in NPS only through an NRE or NRO account.
Investment Options under NPS for NRIs
The National Pension Scheme is known for its investment flexibility as it allows investors to invest in
various assets and securities in the market. Some common examples of investment options under NPS
for NRIs include equities, corporate bonds, government securities, etc. NRIs have the option of investing
in a mix of securities as well, depending on their risk appetite.
Under NPS, there are two types of fund management schemes available for NRI NPS accounts:
● Active Choice: As the name suggests, under this type of fund management scheme, NRIs can
actively decide the type of asset class they wish to invest in and also manage their funds.
● Auto Choice: Under the Auto Choice fund management scheme, the asset investment is done in
a Life-cycle fund in which the proportion of funds are invested across three asset classes
that are determined by a pre-defined portfolio and would change as per the subscribers age.
This type of fund management is for NRIs who do not want to actively engage in investments.
Tax Benefits for NPS for NRIs
The National Pension Scheme comes with several tax benefits for NRIs under the Income Tax Act, 1961.
The easiest way to understand the tax benefits under NPS is to evaluate these benefits under different
types of NPS accounts:
Tier I Account Tax Benefits Under Section 80CCD
● Tax deduction up to 10% of the total salary can be claimed under section 80CCD(1). However,
the maximum limit under this section is ₹1.5 lakhs as per section 80CCE.
● An Additional tax deduction up to ₹50,000 can be claimed under section 80CCD(1B).
Note: Remember that these elections are not allowed under the new tax regime.
Tier II Account Tax Benefits Under Section 80C
● Contributions made towards a Tier II account are eligible for tax deductions under Section 80C.
● The deduction limit of section 80C is ₹1.5 lakh.
● Tax deduction a Tier II account can be claimed only when there is a minimum lock-in period of
three years.
Note: Kindly remember that NRIs who are filing their taxes in India can get tax benefits, as per the tax
regime they choose and the tax slab they fall into.
Withdrawal Rules and Exit Options for NPS for NRIs
The primary purpose of investing in the National Pension Scheme as an NRI is to build a corpus to live a
peaceful life after retirement. Therefore, it is important that you first carefully understand the NPS
withdrawal rules for NRI and exit options under the scheme.
Withdrawal at the Age of 60 Years
● If the corpus (on the date of initiation of the exit request) is over ₹5,00,000, then the subscriber
can draw up to 60% as a lump sum amount.
● The remaining 40% has to be mandatorily invested in buying an annuity.
● In case the total savings amount in the NPS account is less than ₹5,00,000, then the entire
amount can be withdrawn at once.
Note: The subscriber can stay invested in the account till the age of 70 years.
Withdrawal Before the Age of 60 Years
● In case of a premature exit, a partial withdrawal of only 20% of the total amount is allowed. The
remaining 80% of the corpus has to be used for annuity.
● If the corpus amount is less than ₹2.5 lakhs, then a 100% withdrawal can be made.
Death of the Subscriber
● In case of the death or the subscriber before the maturity period, the appointed nominee/ legal
heir would receive the entire accrued pension corpus.
Key Takeaways
● The National Pension Scheme is a government-backed retirement plan that is regulated by the
Pension Fund Regulatory and Development Authority.
● The scheme is available for both resident and non-resident Indians between the ages of 18 and
70 years.
● Under NPS, there are two types of accounts: Tier I and Tier II.
● There are different investment options available for the NRIs under NPS, like equities, corporate
bonds, government securities, etc.
● A partial withdrawal before the age of 60 years is allowed only under specific conditions.
Conclusion
The National Pension Scheme is a reliable option for NRIs as it comes with long-term investment
options, flexibility and a low minimum investment requirement. As an NRI, several options in India can
help juggle financial responsibilities across borders, like term insurance for NRIs or other life insurance
plans.
FAQs
1. Can NRIs open an NPS account without an NRE or NRO account?
No, NRIs cannot open an NPS account without an NRE or NRO account.
2. Is a tier II NPS account available for NRIs?
Yes, the tier II NPS account is available for NRIs who already have an active tier I account under
the National Pension Scheme.
3. What happens to my NPS account if I become an OCI?
The National Pension Scheme is available for Overseas Citizens of India (OCI).
4. Is there any penalty for early withdrawal from NPS for NRIs?
There is no processing charge for exit or withdrawal on the NPS withdrawal.
5. How can NRIs contribute to NPS from abroad?
NRIs can contribute to NPS from abroad simply by using either their Non-Resident External
(NRE) or Non-Resident Ordinary (NRO) account.
6. What are the tax implications of NPS corpus withdrawal for NRIs?
NPS corpus withdrawals are tax-free if withdrawn upon superannuation. However, the annuity
purchased either before or after superannuation is liable for tax liabilities.

changes.word_original: As an NRI, opening an NPS account is a simple process that can be done online.
changes.pdf_final: As an NRI, opening an NPS account is a simple process that can be done through eNPS using their

changes.word_original: Here’s how NRIs can open an NPS account:
changes.pdf_final: Aadhaar Card/ PAN and bank account verification.

changes.word_original: Step 1: Visit the official website of the Pension Fund Regulatory and Development Authority (PFRDA).
changes.pdf_final: An Aadhaar Card holder aged 18 to 60, with their

changes.word_original: Step 2: From the website home page, select the eNPS menu to initiate the registration process.
changes.pdf_final: mobile number registered with Aadhaar can open an NPS online.

changes.word_original: Step 3: On the new page, select the “Non Resident Indian” option and choose between Non Resident External Account (NRE) or Non Resident Ordinary Account (NRO), along with certain other details like country of residence, bank account number, etc.
changes.pdf_final: Here is the step-by-step process.

changes.word_original: Submit these details.
changes.pdf_final: ● Step 1: Getting Started

changes.word_original: Step 4: Upon submission, an NPS registration form appears on the screen.
changes.pdf_final: Visit the official website of the Pension Fund Regulatory and Development Authority (PFRDA).

changes.word_original: Fill in the form with personal details like name, age, etc.
changes.pdf_final: Choose eNPS”.

changes.word_original: Step 5: Submit the form along with all the required documents, including a passport-size photograph and signature.
changes.pdf_final: Click on the “Registration and the “New Registration”.

changes.word_original: Step 6: Complete the process by making the initial payment of ₹500.
changes.pdf_final: Enter passport and

changes.word_original: Once the payment is complete, a unique Permanent Retirement Account Number (PRAN) is generated.
changes.pdf_final: Aadhaar details, verify with OTP, and provide NRE/NRO bank details (via empanelled bank for

changes.word_original: Step 7: The registration process is complete.

changes.word_original: Make sure to print out the system-generated form and send it to the Central Record Keeping Agency within 90 days to avoid freezing of the NPS account for NRI.
changes.pdf_final: Aadhaar will auto-fill your photo
and personal information, and you just need to complete the remaining fields, as explained
below.
● Step 2: Personal Details
Enter your name, parents’ name, mobile number, and email ID.
Your Aadhaar will serve as ID
proof; you just need to add your date of birth and select proof.
Click ‘Generate
Acknowledgement No’ to proceed immediately or use it later to continue.
● Step 3: Contact Details
Your address will be fetched from the Aadhaar details.
Click on save and proceed.
● Step 4: Bank and Other Details
You now need to choose your occupation and fill in the required details, then enter your bank
account information, ensuring the bank name matches the one selected earlier.
For repatriable
accounts, pick from the empanelled banks and provide NRE/NRO details, while for non-
repatriable accounts, you can give any NRE/NRO details on a self-declaration basis.
Finally, click
“Save & Proceed” to continue.
● Step 5: Scheme Nomination & Details
Now you need to make your investment choice: Active or Auto.
Carefully specify the allocation
percentage.
For nominee details, you can add names of up to 3 nominees.
Save and Proceed.
● Step 6: Photograph & Signatures
You have the option of choosing a photograph other than the Aadhaar database.
You would
have to upload the signature and photograph in .jpg format (between 4kb and 12 kb).
Click on
save.
Step 7: Payment
Now you need an initial contribution that can be a minimum of ₹500.
Once the payment is made, you
will receive the Permanent Retirement Account Number (PRAN).
Note: For a repatriable eNPS NRI account, all future contributions must come from the bank account
given at registration.
For a non-repatriable account, contributions can be made through any mode, net
banking, credit card, or debit card.

changes.word_original: The National Pension Scheme is known for its investment flexibility as it allows investors to invest in various assets and securities in the market.
changes.pdf_final: The National Pension Scheme is known for its investment flexibility as it allows investors to invest in

changes.word_original: Some common examples of investment options under NPS for NRIs include equities, corporate bonds, government securities, etc.
changes.pdf_final: various assets and securities in the market.

changes.word_original: NRIs have the option of investing in a mix of securities as well, depending on their risk appetite.
changes.pdf_final: Some common examples of investment options under NPS
for NRIs include equities, corporate bonds, government securities, etc.
NRIs have the option of investing
in a mix of securities as well, depending on their risk appetite.

changes.word_original: Active Choice: As the name suggests, under this type of fund management scheme, NRIs can actively decide the type of asset class they wish to invest in and also manage their funds.
changes.pdf_final: ● Active Choice: As the name suggests, under this type of fund management scheme, NRIs can

changes.word_original: Auto Choice: Under the Auto Choice fund management scheme, the asset investment is automatically done based on factors like the subscriber's age and investment amount made by the subscriber.
changes.pdf_final: actively decide the type of asset class they wish to invest in and also manage their funds.

changes.word_original: This type of fund management is an ideal choice for NRIs who do not want to actively engage in investments.
changes.pdf_final: ● Auto Choice: Under the Auto Choice fund management scheme, the asset investment is done in
a Life-cycle fund in which the proportion of funds are invested across three asset classes
that are determined by a pre-defined portfolio and would change as per the subscribers age.
This type of fund management is for NRIs who do not want to actively engage in investments.

changes.word_original: Contributions made towards this type of account are eligible for tax deductions under Section 80C.
